preFix.title: Using a clearer board to clear a super
preFix.subtitle: The 24-hour step that makes honey harvest easier.
preFix.excerpt: Trying to brush thousands of bees off frames is slow and makes the colony defensive. A clearer board does the same job overnight with no disturbance. How to fit one, what to check when you return, and what to do when the super is still full of bees the next day.
preFix.sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), section on honey harvesting. British Beekeepers Association (BBKA) technical guide on honey extraction. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, chapter on taking surplus honey. BBKA Bee Craft archive, summer harvesting guidance.

content.text: Clearing a
super
before harvest is the step that turns a bruising two-person job into a quiet solo one. A
clearer board
sits between the supers and the
brood box
for 24 to 48 hours. The
porter escape
in its centre lets bees move down into the brood box but not back up. Return the next day and the super will be clear.
When to use a clearer board

Fit a clearer board the evening before you plan to harvest, or at least 24 hours ahead. The timing matters: bees need the night to move down, and you want the super empty before the morning foraging flight begins. Mid-summer is the usual window, once the main nectar flow is slowing and the frames are capped.

Do not fit a clearer board if there is still an active flow on. Bees need access to storage space while nectar is coming in, and sealing them below the super stops them curing the fresh nectar properly. Wait until a week or two after the flow drops off.
What you need
A clearer board with a functioning porter escape fitted and the springs checked
Hive tool, J-type, for breaking the propolis seal between boxes
Lit smoker with dry fuel
Full bee suit and gloves

attrs.body: Press the two springs together with a fingernail. They should snap back to a gap of about 4.5 mm, wide enough for a worker (4 mm thorax) but not wide enough for her to return once the spring closes behind her. If the springs are bent, damaged, or stuck open, bees will move freely in both directions and the board will not clear. Replace the escape before use.
attrs.title: Check the porter escape before fitting

content.text: Setting up at the hive
Light the smoker and suit up. Approach from the side, not the front entrance, and give two or three puffs of smoke under the roof before you lift it. Keep movements slow and deliberate throughout.
Fitting the clearer board
Remove the roof and set it upside down beside the hive. Lift the
crown board
clear and lean it against the hive stand. Take one side of the
propolis

seal with the hive tool and work gently around the super until it lifts free. Slide the clearer board in between the super and the brood box, porter escape facing downward so bees move from super to brood box. Replace the super on top of the clearer board, then the crown board and roof.
Make sure the escape is centred and nothing is blocking it. A frame of burr comb resting against the board can wedge the springs open.
Returning after 24 to 48 hours

Return at a warm part of the day when foragers are out. The super should be quiet. Lift the roof, remove the crown board, and take the super off whole. If the colony is calm and the super is light with no bees visible on the comb surfaces, you are ready to extract. If there are still bees clustered inside, leave the board in place for another 24 hours.
Do not leave the board in place for more than 3 days in warm weather. Bees below will start to move upward to reach their winter stores.

items.fix: Remove the board, check the springs, replace or repair the escape, and refit for another 24 hours. Check that no comb or debris is pressing against the springs from inside the board.
items.cause: Porter escape springs are damaged or blocked; bees are moving freely in both directions
items.symptom: Super still full of bees after 48 hours

items.fix: Wait until the next morning when the cluster has dispersed. If congestion is chronic, add a second brood box before the next season.
items.cause: The brood box is congested and the colony has no space below the escape
items.symptom: Bees clustering between the clearer board and the top of the brood box

items.fix: Fit the clearer board for no more than 24 hours in this situation and harvest the following morning.
items.cause: The colony is restless because the flow has dropped sharply and foragers cannot reach stored honey above the board
items.symptom: Bees exiting the hive in unusually high numbers at the entrance
